title: Limits at Infinity and Horizontal Asymptotes
objectives: Define limits at infinity and distinguish them from infinite limits
Evaluate limits at infinity for rational functions using the divide-by-highest-power technique
Determine horizontal and oblique asymptotes of rational and non-rational functions
Apply the dominance hierarchy to compare growth rates of logarithmic, polynomial, and exponential functions

devotional.title: Eternity in the Heart
devotional.content: "He has made everything beautiful in its time. He has also set eternity in the human heart; yet no one can fathom what God has done from beginning to end." The infinite has always stirred the human spirit. As David Hilbert observed, "No other question has ever moved so profoundly the spirit of man." In this section, we explore what happens as we journey toward infinity along the number line—and discover that some functions settle into a peaceful equilibrium, approaching a finite limit like a horizon that draws ever nearer yet remains always ahead. This mathematical echo of eternity reminds us that God has woven the infinite into the fabric of creation and into our very hearts.

content.text: In Section 2.4, we studied what happens when a function's *output* blows up—the values escape to infinity near a finite trouble spot. Now we ask the complementary question: what happens to the output when the *input* goes to infinity? If we travel far, far along the $x$-axis, does the function eventually settle down?
Let us be perfectly clear about the distinction, because students often confuse these two ideas:

content.title: The Two Infinities — Do Not Confuse Them!
content.content:
**Infinite limits** (Section 2.4):

$x \to a$ (input approaches a finite number)

$f(x) \to \pm\infty$ (output blows up)

Graphical signature: **vertical asymptote** at $x = a$.

**Limits at infinity** (this section):

$x \to \pm\infty$ (input goes to infinity)

$f(x) \to L$ (output settles down to a finite number)

Graphical signature: **horizontal asymptote** at $y = L$.

**Memory aid:** "Infinite limit" = *infinite output*. "Limit at infinity" = *at infinite input*. The word *at* tells you which quantity is going to infinity.

content.text: Consider the function $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$. We already know (from Section 2.4) that $f$ has a vertical asymptote at $x = 0$. But what happens on the other end—as $x$ grows very large?

As $x$ grows without bound in either direction, $f(x)$ approaches $0$. The function settles down; the line $y = 0$ acts like a "landing strip" that the graph approaches but never quite reaches. We write:

content.title: Limit at Infinity
content.content:
Let $f$ be a function defined on some interval $(a, \infty)$. We write

$$\lim_{x \to \infty} f(x) = L$$

if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists a number $N$ such that

$$x > N \implies |f(x) - L| < \varepsilon.$$

In words: we can make $f(x)$ as close to $L$ as we wish by taking $x$ sufficiently large.

Similarly, $\lim_{x \to -\infty} f(x) = L$ means that for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists a number $N$ such that $x < N \implies |f(x) - L| < \varepsilon$.

content.title: Horizontal Asymptote
content.content:
The line $y = L$ is a **horizontal asymptote** of the graph of $f$ if

$$\lim_{x \to \infty} f(x) = L \qquad \text{or} \qquad \lim_{x \to -\infty} f(x) = L \qquad \text{(or both).}$$

items: A function can have *at most two* horizontal asymptotes—one as $x \to \infty$ and one as $x \to -\infty$. (It could have zero, one, or two.) This contrasts with vertical asymptotes, of which a function may have infinitely many (consider $\tan x$).
A function *can* cross a horizontal asymptote. The HA describes *end behavior*—what happens far out along the $x$-axis. Near the origin, the function may cross the line $y = L$ freely.

content.text: The following result is the key to evaluating almost every limit at infinity for rational functions:

content.title: Limits of $1/x^n$ at Infinity
content.content:
For any positive integer $n$,

$$\lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{1}{x^n} = 0 \qquad \text{and} \qquad \lim_{x \to -\infty} \frac{1}{x^n} = 0.$$

More generally, for any positive real number $r$, $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{x^r} = 0$.

content.text: This theorem, combined with limit laws (which apply to limits at infinity just as they do to ordinary limits), is the basis of the master technique for rational functions.
The Master Technique: Divide by the Highest Power

content.title: Limits at Infinity for Rational Functions
content.content: To evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{p(x)}{q(x)}$ where $p$ and $q$ are polynomials, **divide every term in the numerator and denominator by the highest power of $x$ appearing in the denominator.** Then apply Theorem 2.11: every term with $x$ in the denominator vanishes, and you are left with the answer.

content.text: This technique yields a clean rule that depends only on comparing the *degrees* of the numerator and denominator:

content.title: Horizontal Asymptotes of Rational Functions
content.content:
Let $f(x) = \dfrac{a_n x^n + \cdots + a_0}{b_m x^m + \cdots + b_0}$ with $a_n \ne 0$ and $b_m \ne 0$.

**(i)** If $n < m$ (degree of numerator $<$ degree of denominator), then
$$\lim_{x \to \pm\infty} f(x) = 0.$$
Horizontal asymptote: $y = 0$.

**(ii)** If $n = m$ (degrees equal), then
$$\lim_{x \to \pm\infty} f(x) = \frac{a_n}{b_m}.$$
Horizontal asymptote: $y = a_n / b_m$ (ratio of leading coefficients).

**(iii)** If $n > m$ (degree of numerator $>$ degree of denominator), then
$$\lim_{x \to \pm\infty} f(x) = \pm\infty.$$
No horizontal asymptote. The function grows without bound.

content.title: The Rule Is a Shortcut, Not a Replacement
content.content: The degree-comparison rule in Theorem 2.12 is a wonderful shortcut, but you should understand *why* it works (the divide-by-highest-power technique) and be able to carry out the full calculation when asked. On exams, you may be required to show the work, not just state the rule.

content.title: Degree of Numerator Less Than Denominator
content.problem: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{2x + 5}{x^2 - 3}$.
content.solution:
The highest power of $x$ in the denominator is $x^2$. Divide every term by $x^2$:

$$\frac{2x + 5}{x^2 - 3} = \frac{\dfrac{2x}{x^2} + \dfrac{5}{x^2}}{\dfrac{x^2}{x^2} - \dfrac{3}{x^2}} = \frac{\dfrac{2}{x} + \dfrac{5}{x^2}}{1 - \dfrac{3}{x^2}}.$$

As $x \to \infty$, each term with $x$ in the denominator approaches $0$:

$$\lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{\dfrac{2}{x} + \dfrac{5}{x^2}}{1 - \dfrac{3}{x^2}} = \frac{0 + 0}{1 - 0} = 0.$$

This confirms case (i) of Theorem 2.12: degree $1 <$ degree $2$, so the horizontal asymptote is $y = 0$.

content.title: Equal Degrees — The Most Common Case
content.problem: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{3x^2 + 1}{5x^2 - 2}$.
content.solution:
The highest power of $x$ in the denominator is $x^2$. Divide every term by $x^2$:

$$\frac{3x^2 + 1}{5x^2 - 2} = \frac{3 + \dfrac{1}{x^2}}{5 - \dfrac{2}{x^2}}.$$

As $x \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{3 + \dfrac{1}{x^2}}{5 - \dfrac{2}{x^2}} = \frac{3 + 0}{5 - 0} = \frac{3}{5}.$$

The horizontal asymptote is $y = 3/5$. Note that this is simply the ratio of the leading coefficients, exactly as Theorem 2.12(ii) predicts.

The same computation works for $x \to -\infty$, since $1/x^2 \to 0$ regardless of the sign of $x$. So the horizontal asymptote is the same on both sides.

content.title: Degree of Numerator Greater Than Denominator
content.problem: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{x^3 - 2x}{4x^2 + 1}$.
content.solution:
Divide every term by $x^2$ (the highest power in the denominator):

$$\frac{x^3 - 2x}{4x^2 + 1} = \frac{x - \dfrac{2}{x}}{4 + \dfrac{1}{x^2}}.$$

As $x \to \infty$, the numerator $\to \infty$ while the denominator $\to 4$. Therefore:

$$\lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{x^3 - 2x}{4x^2 + 1} = \infty.$$

There is no horizontal asymptote. The function grows without bound as $x \to \infty$.

content.title: Both Vertical and Horizontal Asymptotes
content.problem: Find *all* asymptotes of $f(x) = \dfrac{2x - 1}{x + 3}$ and evaluate the relevant limits.
content.solution:
**Horizontal asymptote:** The numerator and denominator both have degree $1$. By Theorem 2.12(ii):

$$\lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{2x - 1}{x + 3} = \frac{2}{1} = 2, \qquad \lim_{x \to -\infty} \frac{2x - 1}{x + 3} = 2.$$

Horizontal asymptote: $y = 2$.

**Vertical asymptote:** The denominator is zero at $x = -3$, and the numerator $2(-3) - 1 = -7 \ne 0$, so $x = -3$ is a vertical asymptote.

- As $x \to -3^+$: $(x + 3) \to 0^+$ and numerator $\to -7$. So $f(x) \to \dfrac{-7}{0^+} = -\infty$.
- As $x \to -3^-$: $(x + 3) \to 0^-$ and numerator $\to -7$. So $f(x) \to \dfrac{-7}{0^-} = +\infty$.

This function has one vertical asymptote ($x = -3$) and one horizontal asymptote ($y = 2$). The graph approaches the horizontal line $y = 2$ far to the left and far to the right, while blowing up near $x = -3$.

content.title: Pierre de Fermat (1601–1665)
content.content: Long before the formal development of limits, Pierre de Fermat—a French lawyer and amateur mathematician—developed methods for finding tangent lines and maxima/minima that implicitly used limiting processes. Fermat also studied the curve $y = 1/x$ (the "hyperbola of Fermat") and investigated the area under it, discovering what we now recognize as the connection between the hyperbola and the natural logarithm. His work on the long-run behavior of curves anticipated the modern study of asymptotes. Fermat was a devout Catholic who saw mathematics as a noble pursuit of truth, famously scribbling his most tantalizing conjecture—Fermat's Last Theorem—in the margin of his copy of Diophantus.

content.title: A Limit at Infinity Involving a Square Root
content.problem: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty} \bigl(\sqrt{x^2 + 4x} - x\bigr)$.
content.solution:
This is an "$\infty - \infty$" indeterminate form. As $x \to \infty$, both $\sqrt{x^2 + 4x}$ and $x$ go to infinity, so we cannot simply subtract.

The technique is to **rationalize**—multiply and divide by the conjugate:

$$\sqrt{x^2 + 4x} - x = \frac{\bigl(\sqrt{x^2 + 4x} - x\bigr)\bigl(\sqrt{x^2 + 4x} + x\bigr)}{\sqrt{x^2 + 4x} + x} = \frac{(x^2 + 4x) - x^2}{\sqrt{x^2 + 4x} + x} = \frac{4x}{\sqrt{x^2 + 4x} + x}.$$

Now divide numerator and denominator by $x$ (which is positive for $x \to \infty$):

$$= \frac{4}{\dfrac{\sqrt{x^2 + 4x}}{x} + 1} = \frac{4}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{4}{x}} + 1}.$$

As $x \to \infty$, $\dfrac{4}{x} \to 0$, so:

$$\lim_{x \to \infty} \bigl(\sqrt{x^2 + 4x} - x\bigr) = \frac{4}{\sqrt{1 + 0} + 1} = \frac{4}{2} = 2.$$

content.title: Exponential Decay
content.problem: Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty} (3 + 5e^{-x})$.
content.solution:
Recall that $e^{-x} = \dfrac{1}{e^x}$. As $x \to \infty$, $e^x$ grows without bound, so $e^{-x} \to 0$. Therefore:

$$\lim_{x \to \infty} (3 + 5e^{-x}) = 3 + 5(0) = 3.$$

The line $y = 3$ is a horizontal asymptote. Physically, if this function models a cooling process, the object approaches a steady-state temperature of $3$ (in whatever units).

What about $x \to -\infty$? As $x \to -\infty$, $e^{-x} = e^{|x|} \to \infty$, so $3 + 5e^{-x} \to \infty$. There is no horizontal asymptote on the left—the function grows without bound. This function has *different* behavior at $+\infty$ and $-\infty$, which is why we must check both directions.

content.title: A Slant (Oblique) Asymptote
content.problem: Find all asymptotes of $f(x) = \dfrac{x^2 + 2x + 1}{x - 1}$.
content.solution:
**Vertical asymptote:** The denominator is zero at $x = 1$, and $p(1) = 1 + 2 + 1 = 4 \ne 0$, so $x = 1$ is a vertical asymptote.

**Horizontal asymptote:** The degree of the numerator ($2$) is greater than the degree of the denominator ($1$), so by Theorem 2.12(iii), there is *no* horizontal asymptote.

But something more interesting is happening. When the degree of the numerator exceeds the degree of the denominator by *exactly one*, the graph approaches a *slant* (oblique) asymptote—a line $y = mx + b$.

To find it, perform polynomial long division:

$$\frac{x^2 + 2x + 1}{x - 1} = x + 3 + \frac{4}{x - 1}.$$

As $x \to \pm\infty$, the remainder term $\dfrac{4}{x - 1} \to 0$, so

$$f(x) \approx x + 3 \qquad \text{for large } |x|.$$

The line $y = x + 3$ is a **slant asymptote**. The graph of $f$ approaches this line as $x \to \pm\infty$, getting ever closer but never quite coinciding.

**Summary of asymptotes:**
- Vertical asymptote: $x = 1$
- Slant asymptote: $y = x + 3$
- No horizontal asymptote

content.title: Complete Asymptote Analysis
content.problem: Find all asymptotes of $f(x) = \dfrac{3x^2 - 12}{x^2 - x - 2}$ and describe the end behavior.
content.solution:
Factor:

$$f(x) = \frac{3(x^2 - 4)}{(x - 2)(x + 1)} = \frac{3(x - 2)(x + 2)}{(x - 2)(x + 1)}.$$

Cancel $(x - 2)$:

$$f(x) = \frac{3(x + 2)}{x + 1}, \qquad x \ne 2.$$

**Hole:** At $x = 2$, the common factor was cancelled. The $y$-value of the hole: $\dfrac{3(4)}{3} = 4$. Hole at $(2, 4)$.

**Vertical asymptote:** At $x = -1$: numerator $= 3(-1 + 2) = 3 \ne 0$. So $x = -1$ is a vertical asymptote.

- $x \to -1^+$: $(x + 1) \to 0^+$, so $f(x) \to 3/0^+ = +\infty$.
- $x \to -1^-$: $(x + 1) \to 0^-$, so $f(x) \to 3/0^- = -\infty$.

**Horizontal asymptote:** In the simplified form, both numerator and denominator have degree $1$. The leading coefficients are $3$ and $1$.

$$\lim_{x \to \pm\infty} \frac{3(x + 2)}{x + 1} = \frac{3}{1} = 3.$$

Horizontal asymptote: $y = 3$.

**Summary:**
- Hole at $(2, 4)$
- Vertical asymptote: $x = -1$
- Horizontal asymptote: $y = 3$

content.text: When dealing with limits at infinity for non-rational functions, it helps to know which functions "grow fastest." As $x \to \infty$:
where $\ll$ means "grows much slower than." More precisely:

content.title: Exponential Dominates Polynomial
content.content:
For any positive integer $n$,

$$\lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{x^n}{e^x} = 0.$$

Exponential growth eventually overwhelms any polynomial growth.

content.title: Polynomial Dominates Logarithmic
content.content:
For any positive real numbers $r$ and $s$,

$$\lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{(\ln x)^s}{x^r} = 0.$$

Even a small polynomial power eventually overwhelms any power of the logarithm.

content.text: These results will be proved rigorously using L'Hôpital's Rule in Chapter 4, but they are intuitive: the exponential function $e^x$ grows so aggressively that no polynomial can keep up, and the logarithm $\ln x$ grows so sluggishly that any positive power of $x$ eventually dwarfs it.

content.title: John Wallis (1616–1703)
content.content:
The symbol $\infty$ for infinity was introduced by the English mathematician John Wallis in his 1655 work *Tractatus de Sectionibus Conicis*. An ordained priest in the Church of England and Savilian Professor of Geometry at Oxford, Wallis also contributed the foundational ideas that led to the calculus of Newton and Leibniz. His infinite product formula

$$\frac{\pi}{2} = \frac{2}{1} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{5} \cdot \frac{6}{7} \cdots$$

remains one of the most beautiful results connecting infinity to the finite. Wallis understood that the infinite could be tamed through careful reasoning—a lesson this chapter teaches in the language of limits.

content.text: When the degree of the numerator exceeds the degree of the denominator by exactly one, the function does not have a horizontal asymptote, but it does approach a *line*:

content.title: Oblique (Slant) Asymptote
content.content:
The line $y = mx + b$ (with $m \ne 0$) is an **oblique asymptote** (or **slant asymptote**) of $f$ if

$$\lim_{x \to \infty} \bigl[f(x) - (mx + b)\bigr] = 0 \qquad \text{or} \qquad \lim_{x \to -\infty} \bigl[f(x) - (mx + b)\bigr] = 0.$$

content.title: Finding Slant Asymptotes
content.content:
If $f(x) = \dfrac{p(x)}{q(x)}$ where $\deg(p) = \deg(q) + 1$, perform polynomial long division to write

$$f(x) = (mx + b) + \frac{r(x)}{q(x)},$$

where the degree of the remainder $r(x)$ is less than the degree of $q(x)$. Then $\dfrac{r(x)}{q(x)} \to 0$ as $x \to \pm\infty$, so $y = mx + b$ is the slant asymptote.

content.text: Recall the chapter devotional: the builders of Babel raised their tower toward heaven, but heaven remained above—an asymptote they could approach but never reach. In exactly this way, a horizontal asymptote represents a boundary the function eternally approaches. The outputs $f(x)$ draw closer and closer to $L$ as the input grows without bound, yet there is always a gap, however small. The tower rises forever; heaven remains above.

And so we see two faces of infinity in the calculus of limits. In Section 2.4, the function's values *become* infinite—they escape every bound as we approach a finite trouble spot. Here in Section 2.5, we *travel* toward infinity along the $x$-axis and ask whether the function settles down. Both involve the infinite, but from opposite directions. Together, they give us a complete picture of a function's global behavior: its explosions and its calms, its vertical walls and its distant horizons.

content.problem:
Evaluate each limit. State the horizontal asymptote, if one exists.

**(a)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{3}{x + 1}$

**(b)** $\lim_{x \to -\infty} \dfrac{5x}{2x - 7}$

**(c)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{x^2 + 1}{3x^2 - 5}$

**(d)** $\lim_{x \to -\infty} \dfrac{4x^3}{x^3 + 2x + 1}$

Evaluate each limit by dividing by the highest power of $x$ in the denominator. Show all steps.

**(a)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{7x^2 - 3x + 1}{2x^2 + 5}$

**(b)** $\lim_{x \to -\infty} \dfrac{4x + 9}{x^2 + 1}$

**(c)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{6x^3 + x}{3x^3 - 2x^2 + 4}$

**(d)** $\lim_{x \to -\infty} \dfrac{-x^4 + 3x}{2x^4 + x^2 - 1}$

Find all horizontal asymptotes of each function.

**(a)** $f(x) = \dfrac{x}{x^2 + 1}$

**(b)** $g(x) = \dfrac{2x^2 - 1}{x^2 + 3}$

**(c)** $h(x) = \dfrac{x^3 + 2}{x^2 - 1}$

**(d)** $k(x) = \dfrac{5}{(x - 1)(x + 4)}$

Determine the end behavior of each function. State whether $f(x) \to \infty$, $f(x) \to -\infty$, or $f(x) \to L$ as $x \to \infty$ and as $x \to -\infty$.

**(a)** $f(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - 3x}{2x + 1}$

**(b)** $f(x) = \dfrac{-2x^3 + 1}{x^3 + x}$

**(c)** $f(x) = \dfrac{x + 4}{(x - 2)^2}$

**(d)** $f(x) = \dfrac{3x^4 + 1}{-x^4 + 2}$

For each rational function, state the number of horizontal asymptotes (0, 1, or 2) without computing any limits. Explain your reasoning.

**(a)** $f(x) = \dfrac{x^2}{x^3 + 1}$

**(b)** $g(x) = \dfrac{x^3}{x^2 + 1}$

**(c)** $h(x) = \dfrac{3x - 1}{7x + 2}$

Evaluate each limit.

**(a)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} e^{-x}$

**(b)** $\lim_{x \to -\infty} e^{x}$

**(c)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} (2 + 3e^{-x})$

**(d)** $\lim_{x \to -\infty} (4 - e^{x})$

Evaluate each limit.

**(a)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{x^2}{e^x}$

**(b)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{e^x}{x^{100}}$

**(c)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{\ln x}{x}$

**(d)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{(\ln x)^3}{x^{1/2}}$

Evaluate each limit involving square roots.

**(a)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{\sqrt{x^2 + 1}}{x}$

**(b)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \bigl(\sqrt{x^2 + x} - x\bigr)$ (*Hint:* Rationalize.)

**(c)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{\sqrt{9x^2 + 2}}{x + 1}$

**(d)** $\lim_{x \to -\infty} \dfrac{\sqrt{4x^2 + 1}}{x + 3}$ (*Hint:* For $x < 0$, $\sqrt{x^2} = -x$.)
Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty} \bigl(\sqrt{x^2 + 6x + 2} - x\bigr)$ by rationalizing. Show all steps.

Evaluate each limit.

**(a)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{\sin x}{x}$ (*Hint:* Squeeze Theorem.)

**(b)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{\cos(1/x)}{1}$

**(c)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{x + \sin x}{x}$

For each function, find *all* asymptotes (vertical, horizontal, and slant). State each as an equation.

**(a)** $f(x) = \dfrac{x - 1}{x^2 - 4}$

**(b)** $g(x) = \dfrac{2x^2 + 3x}{x^2 - 9}$

**(c)** $h(x) = \dfrac{x^2}{x - 1}$
Find all asymptotes and holes for $f(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - x - 6}{x^2 - 9}$.
Find all asymptotes of $f(x) = \dfrac{x^3 - 1}{x^2 + x - 2}$, including any slant asymptotes. (*Hint:* Factor and simplify first, then perform long division on the simplified form if needed.)

Find the slant asymptote of each function by performing polynomial long division.

**(a)** $f(x) = \dfrac{x^2 + 3x + 5}{x + 1}$

**(b)** $g(x) = \dfrac{2x^2 - x + 4}{x - 3}$

**(c)** $h(x) = \dfrac{x^3 + 1}{x^2 + 2}$
Find all asymptotes (vertical, horizontal, or slant) of $f(x) = \dfrac{2x^2 + 5x - 3}{x + 1}$ and sketch the end behavior.
Evaluate $\lim_{x \to -\infty} \bigl(\sqrt{x^2 + 3x} + x\bigr)$. (*Hint:* Be careful with the sign of $x$ when $x < 0$. Note that $\sqrt{x^2} = |x| = -x$ when $x < 0$.)
Find a rational function $f$ such that $f$ has vertical asymptotes at $x = -1$ and $x = 4$, a horizontal asymptote at $y = 2$, and $f(0) = 1$.
Find all horizontal asymptotes of $f(x) = \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{x^2 + 1}}$. Does this function have different horizontal asymptotes as $x \to \infty$ and as $x \to -\infty$?
Evaluate $$\lim_{x \to \infty} \left(\sqrt{x^2 + ax} - \sqrt{x^2 + bx}\right)$$ in terms of the constants $a$ and $b$.

True or false. If false, give a counterexample.

**(a)** A function can cross its horizontal asymptote.

**(b)** A rational function can have three horizontal asymptotes.

**(c)** If $\lim_{x \to \infty} f(x) = 3$, then $f(x) \ne 3$ for all $x$.

**(d)** If $f$ has a horizontal asymptote at $y = L$, then $f$ has no vertical asymptotes.

**(e)** If $\lim_{x \to \infty} f(x) = \infty$, then $f$ has no horizontal asymptote.
Let $f(x) = \dfrac{x^2 \sin(1/x)}{x + 1}$. Find $\lim_{x \to \infty} f(x)$ if it exists, or explain why it does not.
Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty} \left(\sqrt[3]{x^3 + 3x^2} - x\right)$. (*Hint:* Use the identity $a^3 - b^3 = (a - b)(a^2 + ab + b^2)$ with $a = \sqrt[3]{x^3 + 3x^2}$ and $b = x$.)
Let $f(x) = x \sin\!\left(\dfrac{1}{x}\right)$. Find $\lim_{x \to \infty} f(x)$.
Use the formal definition to prove that $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{x} = 0$.
Prove that $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{x^2} = 0$ directly from the definition, then explain how the general case $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{x^n} = 0$ follows.
Prove Theorem 2.16 part (ii): if $f(x) = \dfrac{a_n x^n + \cdots + a_0}{b_n x^n + \cdots + b_0}$ with $a_n, b_n \ne 0$, then $\lim_{x \to \infty} f(x) = \dfrac{a_n}{b_n}$.
Prove that if $\lim_{x \to \infty} f(x) = L$ and $\lim_{x \to \infty} g(x) = M$, then $\lim_{x \to \infty} [f(x) + g(x)] = L + M$. (*Hint:* Adapt the proof of the sum rule for ordinary limits.)
Prove that a rational function $\dfrac{p(x)}{q(x)}$ (with $p$ and $q$ having no common factors) has a slant asymptote if and only if $\deg(p) = \deg(q) + 1$.

**Terminal Velocity.** A falling object with air resistance has velocity $v(t) = 50(1 - e^{-0.2t})$ m/s, where $t$ is time in seconds.

**(a)** Find $\lim_{t \to \infty} v(t)$ and interpret physically.

**(b)** How long does it take for the object to reach 90% of its terminal velocity?

**(c)** Sketch the graph of $v(t)$ and identify the horizontal asymptote.

**Population Carrying Capacity.** A population of deer is modeled by $P(t) = \dfrac{800}{1 + 15 e^{-0.3t}}$, where $t$ is in years.

**(a)** Find the initial population $P(0)$.

**(b)** Find $\lim_{t \to \infty} P(t)$ and interpret as the carrying capacity of the environment.

**(c)** Explain why the horizontal asymptote represents a natural boundary set by the ecosystem.

**Drug Concentration.** The concentration of a drug in the bloodstream $t$ hours after injection is modeled by $C(t) = \dfrac{5t}{t^2 + 4}$ mg/L.

**(a)** Find $\lim_{t \to \infty} C(t)$. What does this mean medically?

**(b)** Find the time at which the concentration is maximized. (*Hint:* You may use graphing technology.)

**(c)** Find all asymptotes of $C(t)$.

**The Hubble Constant.** In cosmology, the recessional velocity of a galaxy at distance $d$ is approximately $v = H_0 d$, where $H_0 \approx 70$ km/s/Mpc is the Hubble constant. If a galaxy's observed velocity approaches the speed of light as $d$ increases, the relativistic correction gives $$v(d) = c\left(1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (H_0 d/c)^2}}\right),$$ where $c$ is the speed of light. Find $\lim_{d \to \infty} v(d)$ and interpret. What is the horizontal asymptote?

**Economics — Average Cost.** A company's total cost for producing $x$ units is $C(x) = 8000 + 12x + 0.01x^2$. The average cost per unit is $\bar{C}(x) = C(x)/x$.

**(a)** Find $\lim_{x \to \infty} \bar{C}(x)$.

**(b)** Does $\bar{C}(x)$ have a horizontal asymptote? A slant asymptote?

**(c)** Interpret the result: what happens to the average cost per unit as production increases without bound?
